message:
feat(cli): add ntm personas list/show commands (ntm-8js)

Implement persona discovery and inspection commands:
- `ntm personas list` - List all available personas
- `ntm personas list --agent=claude` - Filter by agent type
- `ntm personas list --tag=design` - Filter by tag
- `ntm personas show <name>` - Show detailed persona info
- `--json` flag for machine-readable output
- Source detection (built-in/user/project)

Note: Pre-existing build errors in mail.go, replay.go, root.go
are unrelated to this change.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"ntm-84t","title":"Implement auto-restart for crashed agents","description":"Automatically restart agents that crash or exit unexpectedly.

## What This Task Does

Add --auto-restart flag to ntm spawn that monitors agents and restarts
them if they crash. This builds on the health check infrastructure.

## Interface

$ ntm spawn myproject --cc=2 --auto-restart
Starting agents with auto-restart enabled...
[Agent cc_1 crashed, restarting in 30s...]
[Agent cc_1 restarted successfully]

Configuration:
[resilience]
auto_restart = true
max_restarts = 3
restart_delay_seconds = 30

## Technical Approach

Option 1: Background goroutine
- Spawn launches a monitor goroutine
- Periodically checks health
- Restarts crashed agents
- Exits when session ends

Option 2: Separate daemon
- ntm spawn --auto-restart starts a background process
- Process monitors and restarts
- Survives terminal close

Recommendation: Start with Option 1 (simpler), evolve to Option 2 if needed.

## Restart Logic

1. Detect crash via health check (exit code != 0, process gone)
2. Check restart count for this agent
3. If under max_restarts:
   a. Wait restart_delay_seconds
   b. Get agent type from pane title
   c. Re-run agent command in same pane
   d. Increment restart count
4. If over max_restarts:
   a. Log failure
   b. Send notification (if enabled)
   c. Stop monitoring this agent

## State Tracking

Need to track per-agent:
- Restart count since session start
- Last restart time
- Original agent command

Store in:
- Memory (simple, lost on ntm exit)
- Tmux environment variable (persists)
- File (overkill for now)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] --auto-restart flag enables monitoring
- [ ] Crashed agents are detected and restarted
- [ ] Restart delay is configurable
- [ ] Max restart limit is enforced
- [ ] Restart count shown in health output
- [ ] Works with existing spawn command

 {"id":"ntm-8h3","title":"EPIC: Automation, Pipelines & Integration","description":"Advanced automation including agent pipelines, webhooks, and external integrations.

## Problem Statement
NTM is currently manual and reactive:
- Each prompt requires explicit user action
- No way to chain agent outputs as inputs to other agents
- No notifications when agents finish or error
- No integration with external tools (CI, Slack, etc.)
- File content must be manually copied into prompts

## Goals
1. Enable pipeline mode (chain agents sequentially)
2. Support webhook/notification on events
3. Easy file content injection into prompts
4. Token/cost estimation before sending expensive prompts

## Success Criteria
- `ntm pipeline` chains agent stages with output passing
- Config supports on_error, on_complete webhooks
- `ntm send --file=path` injects file content
- `ntm send --estimate` shows token/cost estimate

## Pipeline Mode
```bash
ntm pipeline myproject \
  --stage="cc: Design the authentication module" \
  --stage="cod: Implement the design from previous stage" \
  --stage="gmi: Write tests for the implementation"
```

Each stage:
1. Sends prompt to specified agent type
2. Waits for completion (detect idle state)
3. Captures output
4. Passes output as context to next stage

## Webhook Config
```toml
[notifications]
on_error = "curl -X POST https://slack.webhook/..."
on_complete = "terminal-notifier -message 'Done'"
on_rate_limit = "echo 'Rate limited' >> ~/ntm-alerts.log"
```

## File Context
```bash
ntm send myproject --all \
  --file=src/auth.py \
  --file=src/models.py:10-50 \
  "Refactor these files to use dependency injection"
```

Injects files with formatting:
```
# File: src/auth.py
\`\`\`python
<file contents>
\`\`\`
```

## Cost Estimation
- Count tokens in prompt using tiktoken or similar
- Estimate based on known pricing (Claude, Codex, Gemini)
- Show estimate, ask for confirmation on expensive prompts

## User Value
- Sophisticated multi-stage workflows
- Stay informed without constant monitoring
- Faster prompt construction with file injection
- Budget awareness for API-heavy usage","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2025-12-10T02:38:05.291885-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T02:38:05.291885-05:00"}
 {"id":"ntm-8h3.1","title":"File Context Injection (--file flag)","description":"Add --file flag to ntm send to inject file contents into prompts.

## Use Case
Instead of manually copying file contents, specify files to include:
```bash
ntm send myproject --all \
  --file=src/auth.py \
  --file=src/models.py \
  "Refactor these to use dependency injection"
```

## Prompt Injection Format
Files are prepended to prompt with clear formatting:
```
# File: src/auth.py
\`\`\`python
<contents of src/auth.py>
\`\`\`

# File: src/models.py
\`\`\`python
<contents of src/models.py>
\`\`\`

---

Refactor these to use dependency injection
```

## Extended Syntax
```bash
--file=path                    # Entire file
--file=path:10-50              # Lines 10-50
--file=path:10-                # Line 10 to end
--file=path:-50                # First 50 lines
--file=path:func:my_function   # Extract function (future)
```

## Implementation
```go
type FileSpec struct {
    Path      string
    StartLine int  // 0 = from beginning
    EndLine   int  // 0 = to end
}

func ParseFileSpec(spec string) (FileSpec, error) {
    // Parse "path:start-end" format
}

func InjectFiles(specs []FileSpec, prompt string) (string, error) {
    var parts []string
    
    for _, spec := range specs {
        content, err := readFileRange(spec)
        if err != nil {
            return "", fmt.Errorf("failed to read %s: %w", spec.Path, err)
        }
        
        lang := detectLanguage(spec.Path)
        header := fmt.Sprintf("# File: %s", spec.Path)
        if spec.StartLine > 0 || spec.EndLine > 0 {
            header += fmt.Sprintf(" (lines %d-%d)", spec.StartLine, spec.EndLine)
        }
        
        parts = append(parts, fmt.Sprintf("%s\n```%s\n%s\n```", 
            header, lang, content))
    }
    
    parts = append(parts, "---\n\n"+prompt)
    return strings.Join(parts, "\n\n"), nil
}
```

## Language Detection
Map file extensions to markdown language tags:
```go
var langMap = map[string]string{
    ".py":   "python",
    ".go":   "go",
    ".js":   "javascript",
    ".ts":   "typescript",
    ".rs":   "rust",
    ".rb":   "ruby",
    ".java": "java",
    // etc.
}
```

## Integration with Templates
Templates can reference {{file}} and {{files}}:
```markdown
---
name: review
variables:
  file: required
---
Review the following file for issues:

{{file}}

Focus on security and performance.
```

## Flags
| Flag | Description |
|------|-------------|
| -f, --file | File to include (repeatable) |
| --file-format | Format: markdown (default), raw, xml |
| --no-file-header | Omit file path headers |

## Edge Cases
- File not found: Error with clear message
- Binary files: Skip with warning
- Very large files: Warn if > 50KB, --force to override
- Relative vs absolute paths: Resolve relative to cwd

## Files to Modify
- internal/cli/send.go - Add --file flag processing
- internal/prompt/inject.go - File injection logic (new)","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2025-12-10T02:43:58.519465-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T02:43:58.519465-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ntm-8h3.1","depends_on_id":"ntm-8h3","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2025-12-10T02:43:58.520574-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}

## Purpose

Allow users to discover and inspect available personas.

## Commands

```bash
# List all personas
ntm personas list
ntm personas list --json

# Show specific persona
ntm personas show architect
ntm personas show architect --json
```

## List Output

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Available Personas                                         │
├──────────────┬────────────┬────────────────────────────────┤
│ Name         │ Agent      │ Description                    │
├──────────────┼────────────┼────────────────────────────────┤
│ architect    │ claude     │ Senior software architect      │
│ implementer  │ claude     │ Fast implementation engineer   │
│ reviewer     │ claude     │ Meticulous code reviewer       │
│ tester       │ codex      │ Test engineer                  │
│ documenter   │ gemini     │ Documentation writer           │
├──────────────┴────────────┴────────────────────────────────┤
│ Source: 3 built-in, 2 user (~/.config/ntm/personas.toml)   │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## Show Output

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Persona: architect                                         │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Agent Type:   claude                                       │
│ Model:        opus                                         │
│ Temperature:  0.7                                          │
│ Source:       built-in                                     │
│ Tags:         design, review                               │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ System Prompt:                                             │
│                                                            │
│ You are a senior software architect. Focus on:             │
│ - System design and high-level structure                   │
│ - Reviewing and guiding implementation                     │
│ - Identifying architectural risks                          │
│ - NOT writing implementation code (delegate that)          │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Context Files:                                             │
│ - ARCHITECTURE.md                                          │
│ - docs/design/*.md                                         │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## JSON Output

```json
{
  "name": "architect",
  "description": "Senior software architect",
  "agent_type": "claude",
  "model": "opus",
  "temperature": 0.7,
  "system_prompt": "...",
  "context_files": ["ARCHITECTURE.md", "docs/design/*.md"],
  "tags": ["design", "review"],
  "source": "built-in"
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] ntm personas list shows all personas
- [ ] ntm personas show displays details
- [ ] Source indicator (built-in, user, project)
- [ ] --json output for scripts
- [ ] Filtering by tag or agent type","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:23:49.772792-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T04:36:37.596466-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ntm-8js","depends_on_id":"ntm-hzl","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:24:01.048364-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}

 {"id":"ntm-97c5","title":"EPIC: IDE Integration (VS Code)","description":"# IDE Integration (VS Code Extension)

## Background & Motivation

Most developers work primarily in VS Code. IDE integration reduces context switching and enables unique value that terminal-only can't provide.

## CRITICAL: Focus on Unique IDE Value

The terminal TUI is already excellent. Don't rebuild it in VS Code.

**Unique IDE value:**
1. **Editor context injection**: Send selected code/current file to agents
2. **Quick access**: Status bar for at-a-glance info
3. **File decorations**: Show lock status in explorer

**NOT prioritized:**
- Full dashboard webview (duplicates TUI)
- Complex session management (use terminal)

## What the Extension Provides

### 1. Status Bar (High Value, Low Effort)
```
[NTM: myproject ● 4 agents]
```
- Click to open command palette
- Shows session name, agent count
- Color indicates status (green=ok, yellow=warning)

### 2. Commands (High Value, Medium Effort)
```
NTM: Send Selection to Agent
NTM: Send Current File to Agent
NTM: Spawn Agents for Project
NTM: Show Session Status
NTM: Open Terminal (attach to session)
```

### 3. Context Injection (Killer Feature)
```typescript
// When user invokes "Send Selection"
const selection = editor.selection;
const text = editor.document.getText(selection);
const filePath = editor.document.fileName;

// Construct context-aware prompt
const prompt = `
File: ${filePath}
Selected code:
\`\`\`
${text}
\`\`\`

[User's instruction here]
`;

// Send via ntm send
await ntm.send(session, target, prompt);
```

### 4. File Decorations (Medium Value, Medium Effort)
Show lock status in file explorer:
- 🔒 icon for locked files
- Tooltip: "Locked by GreenCastle (cc_1)"
- Requires Agent Mail integration

### 5. Simple Session View (Optional)
Activity bar panel with:
- List of sessions
- Agent count per session
- Quick actions (attach, kill)

NOT a full dashboard - just navigation.

## What We're NOT Building

- ❌ Full webview dashboard (use terminal TUI)
- ❌ Inline agent chat (complex, scope creep)
- ❌ Custom editor for prompts (overkill)

## Technical Approach

### CLI Wrapper
```typescript
class NtmClient {
    async getStatus(): Promise<RobotStatus> {
        const result = await exec('ntm --robot-status');
        return JSON.parse(result.stdout);
    }
    
    async send(session: string, target: string, prompt: string): Promise<void> {
        await exec(`ntm send ${session} ${target} ${quote(prompt)}`);
    }
}
```

### Extension Activation
Activate when:
- Workspace contains .ntm/ directory
- User runs NTM command
- NTM status bar clicked

## Priority Order

1. **P2**: Status bar + basic commands (NTM: Spawn, Send)
2. **P2**: Context injection (Send Selection)
3. **P3**: File decorations (requires Agent Mail)
4. **P3**: Session tree view
5. **P4**: Webview (if ever)

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Status bar shows current session
- [ ] Commands in palette for common actions
- [ ] "Send Selection to Agent" works with context
- [ ] Extension published to marketplace
- [ ] Minimal dependencies, fast activation
- [ ] Degrades gracefully if ntm not installed","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:28:29.775574-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T03:37:02.457909-05:00"}

 {"id":"ntm-je6","title":"EPIC: Git Coordination Layer","description":"# Git Coordination Layer

## Background & Motivation

When multiple agents edit the same codebase concurrently, git conflicts are inevitable:
- Agent A modifies src/api/users.py
- Agent B simultaneously modifies the same file
- One commit overwrites the other, or merge conflicts arise

## Integration Levels

This EPIC has TWO levels:

### Level 1: Basic Git Visibility (No Agent Mail Required)
- `ntm git status`: Show git state per agent pane
- `ntm git sync`: Pull/push across panes
- Works without Agent Mail

### Level 2: Coordinated Locking (Requires Agent Mail)
- `ntm lock`: Reserve files via Agent Mail
- `ntm unlock`: Release reservations
- Conflict detection before spawn
- Pre-commit guard integration

## Commands

### ntm git status (Level 1)
```bash
ntm git status myproject
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Git Status: myproject                                       │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Branch: feature/auth                                        │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Pane      │ Modified │ Staged │ Status                     │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ cc_1      │ 3 files  │ 1 file │ uncommitted changes       │
│ cc_2      │ 0 files  │ 0 files│ clean                      │
│ cod_1     │ 2 files  │ 0 files│ uncommitted changes       │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### ntm git sync (Level 1)
```bash
ntm git sync myproject
# Pulls latest in all panes (with user confirmation)
# Shows conflicts if any

ntm git sync myproject --check
# Preview what would happen, don't execute
```

### ntm lock / ntm unlock (Level 2 - Requires Agent Mail)
```bash
ntm lock myproject "src/api/**" --reason "Refactoring"
ntm unlock myproject --all
ntm locks myproject  # Show all reservations
```

If Agent Mail unavailable:
```
⚠️ File locking requires Agent Mail (not detected)
  Start Agent Mail: mcp-agent-mail serve
  Or continue without locking (not recommended for multi-agent work)
```

## Graceful Degradation

| Feature | Agent Mail Available | Agent Mail Unavailable |
|---------|---------------------|----------------------|
| ntm git status | Full (with locks) | Basic (git only) |
| ntm git sync | Full | Full |
| ntm lock/unlock | Works | Error with help |
| Conflict prevention | Works | Not available |

## Success Criteria

- [ ] ntm git status shows per-pane git state
- [ ] ntm git sync pulls across panes safely
- [ ] Level 2 features require Agent Mail (with helpful errors)
- [ ] Graceful degradation documented

 {"id":"ntm-k7o","title":"Integrate --ssh flag with all session commands","description":"Add --ssh support to spawn, list, attach, send, status, kill.

## What This Task Does

After the SSH wrapper (ntm-5zs) is complete, integrate --ssh flag
with all commands that operate on sessions.

## Commands to Update

- ntm spawn --ssh host: Create session on remote
- ntm list --ssh host: List remote sessions
- ntm attach --ssh host: Attach to remote session
- ntm send --ssh host: Send prompt to remote agents
- ntm status --ssh host: Get remote session status
- ntm kill --ssh host: Kill remote session

## Implementation Pattern

For each command:
1. Add --ssh flag (string, optional)
2. Pass to TmuxClient constructor
3. All tmux operations use client abstraction

func runSpawn(cmd *cobra.Command, args []string) error {
    sshHost, _ := cmd.Flags().GetString("ssh")
    client := tmux.NewClient(sshHost)
    
    // All operations use client
    if err := client.CreateSession(session); err != nil {
        return err
    }
    ...
}

## Session Host Persistence

When spawning remotely, store host in tmux environment:
client.SetEnvironment(session, "NTM_REMOTE_HOST", sshHost)

When operating on existing session without --ssh:
1. Get session's NTM_REMOTE_HOST
2. If set, use it
3. If not, assume local

## Error Handling

- Connection timeout: Clear message, suggest checking connectivity
- Auth failure: Suggest ssh-agent or key path
- Remote tmux not found: Suggest installing tmux on remote

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] --ssh works on spawn
- [ ] --ssh works on list  
- [ ] --ssh works on attach
- [ ] --ssh works on send
- [ ] --ssh works on status
- [ ] --ssh works on kill
- [ ] Remote host auto-detected for existing sessions

 {"id":"ntm-l4fp","title":"Add Agent Mail notifications for scan results","description":"## Overview
Integrate UBS scan results with Agent Mail to notify coordinating agents about code quality findings, enabling proactive collaboration on fixes.

## Background
When multiple AI agents work on a codebase, scan results are valuable coordination signals:
- New critical findings need immediate attention
- Findings in files an agent is editing should be surfaced
- Scan summaries help agents prioritize work

## Implementation Details

### Notification Types
1. **Scan Summary** - After each scan, send digest to all active agents
   - Total findings by severity
   - New vs. existing findings
   - Files with most issues

2. **Targeted Alerts** - Notify specific agents about relevant findings
   - Match findings to file reservations
   - Agent editing auth.go gets auth-related findings
   - Importance: high for critical, normal for others

3. **Fix Confirmations** - When findings are resolved
   - Notify original reporter
   - Update thread if findings were discussed

### Message Templates
```markdown
Subject: [Scan] {n} new findings in {project}

## Scan Summary
- Critical: {critical_count}
- Errors: {error_count}  
- Warnings: {warning_count}

## New Findings
{finding_list}

## Your Files
The following findings affect files you're working on:
{agent_relevant_findings}
```

### Files to Create/Modify
- internal/scanner/notify.go - Notification logic
- internal/scanner/matcher.go - File reservation matching
- internal/cli/scan.go - Add --notify flag

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Scan summaries sent to registered agents
- [ ] Findings matched to file reservations
- [ ] Appropriate importance levels set
- [ ] Notifications are concise and actionable","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:33:31.76544-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T03:38:41.640981-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ntm-l4fp","depends_on_id":"ntm-p77j","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:34:58.079729-05:00","created_by":"daemon"},{"issue_id":"ntm-l4fp","depends_on_id":"ntm-lk66","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:35:13.789853-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}
 {"id":"ntm-l5h","title":"EPIC: Comprehensive Testing Infrastructure","description":"# EPIC: Comprehensive Testing Infrastructure

## Background & Rationale

NTM (Named Tmux Manager) currently has very low test coverage across most packages. This EPIC addresses the need for:

1. **Full Unit Test Coverage** - Testing each function/method in isolation WITHOUT mocks or fakes where possible, using real dependencies
2. **Integration Tests** - Testing component interactions (CLI → tmux, config → commands, etc.)
3. **End-to-End (E2E) Tests** - Full workflow tests with detailed logging for debugging

## Current Coverage Analysis

| Package | Current Coverage | Status |
|---------|-----------------|--------|
| cmd/ntm | 0.0% | 🔴 Critical |
| internal/cli | 0.0% | 🔴 Critical |
| internal/config | 16.2% | 🟡 Needs improvement |
| internal/output | 55.1% | 🟡 Good start |
| internal/palette | 0.0% | 🔴 Critical |
| internal/robot | 0.0% | 🔴 Critical |
| internal/status | 69.6% | 🟢 Good (has failures) |
| internal/tmux | 5.4% | 🔴 Critical |
| internal/tui/* | 0.0% | 🔴 Critical |
| internal/tutorial | 0.0% | 🔴 Critical |
| internal/updater | 0.0% | 🔴 Critical |
| internal/watcher | 0.0% | 🔴 Critical |

## Testing Philosophy

### No Mocks/Fakes Policy
- Use REAL tmux sessions for tmux tests (create/destroy per test)
- Use REAL file system for config tests
- Use REAL HTTP calls where applicable
- Only mock external services that are impractical (GitHub API rate limits, etc.)

### Detailed Logging Strategy
- All E2E tests output structured logs with timestamps
- Log file per test run with full command output
- Capture stderr/stdout separately
- Include system state before/after each test

## Success Criteria
- 80%+ coverage on all non-TUI packages
- 60%+ coverage on TUI packages (harder to test without mocks)
- All E2E tests pass on macOS and Linux
- Tests complete in <5 minutes total
- No flaky tests (3 consecutive passes required)

## Technical Considerations
- Tests requiring tmux must be skipped in CI without tmux
- Use Go's testing.Short() for quick vs full test modes
- Parallelize where safe (different tmux sessions)
- Clean up resources even on test failure","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:17:38.345103-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T03:18:03.068304-05:00"}
